documentation.readme: > :warning: autobloody has been moved to its own repo
usage_sections: Description: This tool can perform specific LDAP calls to a domain controller in order to perform AD privesc. bloodyAD supports authentication using cleartext passwords, pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket or certificates and binds to LDAP services of a domain controller to perform AD privesc. Exc

arguments.name: --target
arguments.description: Target host, IP, MAC, or URL the tool operates on.

arguments.name: --output
arguments.description: Output file path for the report or capture.

arguments.name: --version
arguments.description: Print the tool version and exit.

arguments.name: --wordlist
arguments.description: Path to a wordlist (users, passwords, paths).

arguments.name: --dry-run
arguments.description: Print what would be done without making any changes.

arguments.name: --force
arguments.description: Force the action even if a safety check would block it.

chain_examples.note: Shares attack_surface+phase_hint+tag with zynon/LdapRelayScan (score=10); pair in the chain planner.
Shares attack_surface+phase_hint+tag with swisskyrepo/PayloadsAllTheThings (score=10); pair in the chain planner.
Shares attack_surface+phase_hint+tag with skylot/jadx (score=10); pair in the chain planner.
Phase 3: same attack_surface=['web', 'ad'] and phase_hint='any'; this entry chains with adjacent tools in the same family. The full set of siblings is enumerated by the catalog search index.
Phase 4: same attack_surface=['web', 'ad'] and phase_hint='any'; this entry chains with adjacent tools in the same family. The full set of siblings is enumerated by the catalog search index.
Phase 5: same attack_surface=['web', 'ad'] and phase_hint='any'; this entry chains with adjacent tools in the same family. The full set of siblings is enumerated by the catalog search index.

documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'any' phase against surfaces [web, ad] in the frameworks category. Fits when the engagement needs: > :warning: autobloody has been moved to its own repo.
documentation.why_to_use: Prefer this tool based on upstream description: > :warning: autobloody has been moved to its own repo.
documentation.how_to_use: Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `bloodyAD --host $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -d bloody.local -u jane.doe -p :70016778cb0524c799ac25b439bd6a31 set password=$KFIOSA_SECRET '$KFIOSA_SECRET'`. > :warning: autobloody has been moved to its own repo.

name: bloodyAD
summary: > :warning: autobloody has been moved to its own repo

trust.warning: Attribution / index entry only. The operator must audit the code, the README, the license, and the releases before invoking. Phase 5 — entry is auto-generated from the operator's curated fetch list; no automated enrichment yet.

use_cases: When you need frameworks, use bloodyAD because > :warning: autobloody has been moved to its own repo.

When you need a concrete invocation, run `bloodyAD --host $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST -d bloody.local -u jane.doe -p :70016778cb0524c799ac25b439bd6a31 set password=$KFIOSA_SECRET '$KFIOSA_SECRET'` as documented upstream for bloodyAD.
When chaining frameworks tools, pair bloodyAD with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `bloodyAD --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
